papergraph.title: Pushing Lithium Cobalt Oxides to 4.7 V by Lattice‐Matched Interfacial Engineering
papergraph.citation: Xuerui Yang et al. (2022) Pushing Lithium Cobalt Oxides to 4.7 V by Lattice‐Matched Interfacial Engineering doi:10.1002/aenm.202200197
papergraph.abstract: The utilization of high‐voltage LiCoO2 is imperative to break the bottleneck of the practical energy density of lithium‐ion batteries. However, LiCoO2 suffers from severe structural and interfacial degradation at >4.55 V. Herein, a novel lattice‐matched LiCoPO4 coating is rationally designed for LiCoO2 which works at 4.6 V (vs Li/Li+) or above. This LiCoPO4 coating, derived by an in situ chemical reaction, grows epitaxially on LiCoO2 crystallite with strong bonding and complete coverage to LiCoO2, ensuring a stable cathode–electrolyte interface with fewer side reactions and alleviated intergranular cracking and phase collapse during repeated high‐voltage lithiation/delithiation processes. In addition, the formed strong covalent P–O tetrahedron configuration at the interface effectively decreases the surface oxygen activity of LiCoO2, further suppressing oxygen release and irreversible phase transition. Therefore, the LiCoPO4‐LiCoO2ǁLi cells display excellent capacity retention of 87% after 300 cycles at 4.6 V and stable operation at 4.6 V/55 °C or 4.7 V/30 °C. The strategy of lattice‐matching growth affords a new way to impact the development of high‐voltage LiCoO2 and beyond.

suppressed oxygen release and irreversible phase transition
improvements: capacity retention of 87% after 300 cycles at 4.6 V
stable operation at 4.6 V/55 °C or 4.7 V/30 °C

papergraph.title: Hierarchical Garnet‐Type Interfacial Engineering Enables Reversible Oxygen Redox in Li‐Rich Layered Cathodes
papergraph.citation: Yuxin Zheng et al. (2026) Hierarchical Garnet‐Type Interfacial Engineering Enables Reversible Oxygen Redox in Li‐Rich Layered Cathodes doi:10.1002/adfm.202530416
papergraph.abstract: Li‐rich layered oxides (LROs) are promising high‐capacity cathodes for next‐generation Li‐ion batteries, yet suffer from irreversible oxygen release and interfacial degradation, leading to voltage decay, capacity fading, and gas evolution. Herein, we report an in situ constructed garnet‐type Li5La3Nb2O12 (LLNO) fast‐ion conductor coating on LRO particles via a sol–gel process using C4H4NNbO9·nH2O as the Nb precursor. During pyrolysis, released NH3 acts as a mild reducing agent, promoting surface reconstruction accompanied by near‐surface Nb5+ doping, formation of a spinel transition layer, and a gradient oxygen‐vacancy distribution. The resulting hierarchical architecture integrates: (i) interfacial protection and chemical stability from the LLNO coating, (ii) structural coherence and multidimensional Li+ transport from the spinel layer, and (iii) reversible oxygen‐anion redox stabilization enabled by Nb5+–O bonding and oxygen vacancies. Benefiting from these cooperative effects, the optimized cathode delivers 281.7 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C and maintains 97.6% capacity retention after 200 cycles at 1 C, outperforming the pristine counterpart. This work demonstrates a multifunctional interfacial‐engineering strategy that unifies coating, doping, and surface reconstruction, providing a generalizable pathway toward durable, high‐energy LROs.
papergraph.benchmark: capacity retention | discharge capacity | oxygen redox reversibility
papergraph.contribution: A multifunctional interfacial-engineering strategy unifying coating, doping, and surface reconstruction enables reversible oxygen redox and enhances cycling stability in Li-rich layered cathodes.
papergraph.limitation: Benefiting from these cooperative effects, the optimized cathode delivers 281.7 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C and maintains 97.6% capacity retention after 200 cycles at 1 C, outperforming the pristine counterpart.

papergraph.title: Surface Reconstruction Enables High‐Voltage, Long‐Life All‐Solid‐State Batteries
papergraph.citation: Hang Liu et al. (2025) Surface Reconstruction Enables High‐Voltage, Long‐Life All‐Solid‐State Batteries doi:10.1002/adfm.202516460
papergraph.abstract: All‐solid‐state batteries (ASSBs) offer enhanced energy density and improved safety through the utilization of solid electrolytes. Among these, halide‐based electrolytes have emerged as the most promising candidates due to their high oxidative stability and compatibility with layered oxide cathodes. However, current voltage limitations (≤4.3 V) restrict the advancement of energy density, while elevated cut‐off voltages result in structural degradation of layered oxides and deterioration of the cathode electrolyte interface (CEI). In this study, an atomic‐scale surface reconstruction strategy is presented that transforms the LiCoO2 surface into a perovskite phase nanolayer through limited Li+/La3+ ion exchange. The engineered interface suppresses lattice oxygen release and stabilizes the CEI, thus preventing both bulk‐phase collapse and decomposition of the Li3InCl6 electrolyte. The engineered batteries demonstrate remarkable performance, exhibiting a high specific capacity of 208 mAh g−1 with an initial coulombic efficiency of 97.4% at 4.6 V and impressive long‐term cycling stability (over 1000 cycles). Furthermore, the capacity retention is found to be 94% even after 200 cycles at high loading (5 mAh cm−2). These findings establish surface reconstruction as a promising strategy for the engineering of interfaces in high‐voltage ASSBs, providing insights that will inform the development of next‐generation energy storage systems.

papergraph.title: Subcritical Hydrothermal Synthesis in a Water-Alcohol Co-solvent of Size-Controlled High-Entropy Spinel Oxide Nanoparticles as Oxygen Evolution Electrocatalysts
papergraph.citation: Ryoga Kato et al. (2026) Subcritical Hydrothermal Synthesis in a Water-Alcohol Co-solvent of Size-Controlled High-Entropy Spinel Oxide Nanoparticles as Oxygen Evolution Electrocatalysts doi:10.26434/chemrxiv.15002476/v2
papergraph.abstract: High-entropy oxides (HEOs) composed of more than five transition metal elements are promising noble-metal-free electrocatalysts for sustainable water oxidation. However, the low-temperature synthesis of size-controlled, single-phase, and crystalline HEO nanoparticles remains challenging because multicomponent oxide formation requires simultaneous nucleation and crystallization of multiple metal species with different hydrolysis behaviors. Herein, we report an alcohol-assisted subcritical hydrothermal strategy for synthesizing size-controlled HEO nanoparticles composed of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, and Zn. By tuning the water–alcohol co-solvent composition, the average particle size decreased from 17.2 to 10.0 nm without significant changes in the overall metal composition. Structural characterizations confirmed that the obtained nanoparticles retained a single-phase spinel oxide structure with homogeneous elemental distribution and similar local coordination environments. The size reduction was considered to be attributed to changes in solvent properties, particularly the lowered dielectric constant of the water–alcohol co-solvent, which promotes rapid nucleation and suppresses particle coarsening. The resulting smaller HEO nanoparticles exhibited enhanced alkaline oxygen evolution reaction activity. The nearly unchanged Tafel slopes indicate that the improved activity mainly originates from an increased number of accessible active sites rather than a change in the reaction mechanism. This work provides a simple solution-based route for nanoscale control of crystalline multicomponent oxide electrocatalysts and offers a sustainable design strategy for noble-metal-free water oxidation catalysts.

papergraph.title: Subcritical Hydrothermal Synthesis in a Water-Alcohol Co-solvent of Size-Controlled High-Entropy Spinel Oxide Nanoparticles as Oxygen Evolution Electrocatalysts
papergraph.citation: Ryoga Kato et al. (2026) Subcritical Hydrothermal Synthesis in a Water-Alcohol Co-solvent of Size-Controlled High-Entropy Spinel Oxide Nanoparticles as Oxygen Evolution Electrocatalysts doi:10.26434/chemrxiv.15002476/v1
papergraph.abstract: High-entropy oxides (HEOs) are promising electrocatalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) due to their unique electronic structure caused by the interactions of the multiple constituent metal elements. The particle size of the catalysts is one of important determining parameters of the catalysts’ OER activity. However, synthesizing size-controlled single-phase HEO nanoparticles (NPs) remain challenging issue. Herein, we report an alcohol-assisted subcritical hydrothermal synthesis strategy to synthesize spinel-based HEO NPs with average diameter of approx. 10 nm composed of five first transition metal elements. Mixing methanol to water as solvent reduces the bulk dielectric constant, decreasing precursor solubility and increasing the degree of supersaturation. This change of the property of the solvent induces simultaneous burst nucleation and suppresses Ostwald ripening, thereby preventing particle coarsening. The crystallite size was systematically tuned from 13.3 nm down to 7.5 nm while maintaining their compositions. The OER activity of the catalysts synthesized by different alcohol content was enhanced significantly by reducing the particle size, which causes an increase of the specific surface area of the catalysts. This work establishes a promising route for designing crystalized HEO NPs with high catalytic activity.

papergraph.title: Onset potential for electrolyte oxidation and Ni-rich cathode degradation in lithium-ion batteries
papergraph.citation: Wesley M. Dose et al. (2022) Onset potential for electrolyte oxidation and Ni-rich cathode degradation in lithium-ion batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2022-xxtl8
papergraph.abstract: High-capacity Ni-rich layered metal oxide cathodes are highly desirable to increase the energy density of lithium-ion batteries. However, these materials suffer from poor cycling performance, which is exacerbated by increased cell voltage. We demonstrate here the detrimental effect of ethylene carbonate (EC), a core component in conventional electrolytes, when NMC811 (LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2) is charged above 4.4 V vs. Li/Li+ – the onset potential for lattice oxygen release. Oxygen loss is enhanced by EC-containing electrolytes – compared to EC-free – and correlates with more electrolyte oxidation/breakdown and cathode surface degradation, which increase concurrently above 4.4 V. In contrast, NMC111 (LiNi0.33Mn0.33Co0.33O2), which does not release oxygen up to 4.6 V, shows similar extents of degradation irrespective of the electrolyte. This work highlights the incompatibility between conventional EC-based electrolytes and Ni-rich cathodes (more generally, cathodes that release lattice oxygen such as Li-/Mn-rich and disordered rocksalt cathodes), and motivates further work on wider classes of electrolytes and additives.

papergraph.title: Unraveling the Dynamic Correlations between Transition Metal Migrations and the Oxygen Dimer Formation in the Layered LiCoO2 Cathode
papergraph.citation: Taiping Hu et al. (2023) Unraveling the Dynamic Correlations between Transition Metal Migrations and the Oxygen Dimer Formation in the Layered LiCoO2 Cathode doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2023-r12zw-v2
papergraph.abstract: Expanding the voltage window is a common approach for increasing the practical capacity of the LiCoO2 cathode. However, it brings serious concerns such as structural degradations and the associated oxygen release induced by the transition metal (TM) cation migrations at a highly delithiated state. Therefore, it is crucial to understand the dynamic correlations between the TM cation migrations and the oxygen dimer formation. In this work, machine-learning-potential-assisted molecular dynamics simulations combined with enhanced sampling techniques are performed to resolve the above question using a representative CoO2 model. Our results show that the occurrence of the Co migrations exhibits local characteristics. The formation of two adjacent Co vacancies (the Co vacancy cluster) is necessary for the oxygen dimer generation. We further show that the introduction of the Ti dopant can significantly increase the kinetic barrier of the Co ion migration and thus effectively suppress the formation of the Co vacancy cluster. Overall, our work reveals the atomic-scale dynamic correlations between the TM migrations and the instability of the oxygen sublattice in the cathode material, and provides insights about the mechanism of the dopants promotion on the stability of cathode materials.
papergraph.contribution: Reveals atomic-scale dynamic correlations between transition metal migrations and oxygen sublattice instability; identifies necessity of two adjacent Co vacancies for oxygen dimer formation; demonstrates Ti doping increases kinetic barrier for Co migration and suppresses Co vacancy cluster formation.

papergraph.title: Unraveling the Dynamic Correlations between Transition Metal Migrations and the Oxygen Dimer Formation in the Layered LiCoO2 Cathode
papergraph.citation: Taiping Hu et al. (2023) Unraveling the Dynamic Correlations between Transition Metal Migrations and the Oxygen Dimer Formation in the Layered LiCoO2 Cathode doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2023-r12zw
papergraph.abstract: Expanding the voltage window is a common approach for increasing the practical capacity of the LiCoO2 cathode. However, it brings serious concerns such as structural degradations and the associated oxygen release induced by the transition metal (TM) cation migrations at a highly delithiated state. Therefore, it is crucial to understand the dynamic correlations between the TM cation migrations and the oxygen dimer formation. In this work, machine-learning-potential-assisted molecular dynamics simulations combined with enhanced sampling techniques are performed to resolve the above question using a representative CoO2 model. Our results show that the occurrence of the Co migrations exhibits local characteristics. The formation of two adjacent Co vacancies (the Co vacancy cluster) is necessary for the oxygen dimer generation. We further show that the introduction of the Ti dopant can significantly increase the kinetic barrier of the Co ion migration and thus effectively suppress the formation of the Co vacancy cluster. Overall, our work reveals the atomic-scale dynamic correlations between the TM migrations and the instability of the oxygen sublattice in the cathode material, and provides insights about the mechanism of the dopants promotion on the stability of cathode materials.

papergraph.title: Monitoring electrode/electrolyte interfaces of Li-ion batteries under working conditions: A surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopic study on LiCoO2 composite cathodes
papergraph.citation: Marcel Heber et al. (2021) Monitoring electrode/electrolyte interfaces of Li-ion batteries under working conditions: A surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopic study on LiCoO2 composite cathodes doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2021-13zcn
papergraph.abstract: Lithium-ion batteries are commonly used for electrical energy storage in portable devices and are promising systems for large-scale energy storage. However, their application is still limited due to electrode degradation and stability issues. To enhance the fundamental understanding of electrode degradation we report on the Raman spectroscopic characterization of LiCoO2 cathode materials of working Li-ion batteries. To facilitate the spectroscopic analysis of the SEI (solid electrolyte interface) we apply surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy by using Au nanoparticles coated with a thin SiO2 layer (Au@SiO2). We observe a surface-enhanced Raman signal of Li2CO3 at 1090 cm-1 during electrochemical cycling as an intermediate. Its formation/decomposition highlights the role of Li2CO3 as a component of the SEI on LiCoO2 composite cathodes. Our results demonstrate the potential of Raman spectroscopy to monitor electrode/electrolyte interfaces of lithium-ion batteries under working conditions thus allowing relations between electrochemical performance and structural changes to be established.

papergraph.title: Ultra-high areal capacity Li electrodeposition at metal-solid electrolyte interfaces under minimal stack pressures enabled by interfacial Na-K liquids
papergraph.citation: Richard Park et al. (2022) Ultra-high areal capacity Li electrodeposition at metal-solid electrolyte interfaces under minimal stack pressures enabled by interfacial Na-K liquids doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2022-93lw0
papergraph.abstract: The need for higher energy density rechargeable batteries has generated interest in metallic electrodes paired with solid electrolytes. However, impedance growth at the Li metal-solid electrolytes interface due to void formation during cycling at practical current densities and areal capacities, e.g., greater than 0.5 mA cm-2 and 1.5 mAh cm-2 respectively, remains a significant barrier. Here, we show that introducing a wetting interfacial film of Na-K liquid between Li metal and Li6.75La3Zr1.75Ta0.25O12 (LLZTO) solid electrolyte permits reversible stripping and plating of up to 150μm of Li (30 mAhcm-2), approximately ten times the areal capacity of today’s lithium-ion batteries, at current densities above 0.5 mA cm-2 and stack pressures below 75 kPa, all with minimal changes in cell impedance. We further show that this increase in the accessible areal capacity at high stripping current densities is due to the presence of Na-K liquid at the Li stripping interface; this performance improvement is not enabled in the absence of the Na-K liquid. This design approach holds promise for overcoming interfacial stability issues that have heretofore limited performance of solid-state metal batteries.

papergraph.title: Dynamic surface reconstruction of perovskite oxides in oxygen evolution reaction and its impacts on catalysis: a critical review
papergraph.citation: Zhonghui Hu et al. (2023) Dynamic surface reconstruction of perovskite oxides in oxygen evolution reaction and its impacts on catalysis: a critical review doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2023-d1bhk
papergraph.abstract: Perovskite oxides have gained significant attention in recent years due to their superior catalytic activity in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). However, the identification of the active structure and corresponding catalytic mechanism for these oxides still remains elusive, particularly for complex perovskite oxides. This is due to the intricate surface reconstruction that may occur under working conditions and the differing reaction mechanisms. This paper aims to address these issues by providing an overview of OER, perovskite oxides, characterization methods, and factors affecting surface reconstruction during OER. Specifically, the paper reviews in-situ and operando characterization studies conducted over the last decade, focusing on the surface dynamic structural evolution of simple and complex perovskite oxides such as SrIrO3 and Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ (BSCF). The paper concludes by presenting an overview of reported active structures on perovskite oxides so far, and providing guiding principles for the design of highly active and stable catalysts. By addressing these issues, this paper provides valuable insights into the design and development of effective catalysts for OER.

papergraph.title: Optimizing the electrochemical performance of LiCoO2 at 4.5 V via synergistic modification of Mg2+ ion doping and Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 coating
papergraph.citation: Gaoqiang Mao et al. (2025) Optimizing the electrochemical performance of LiCoO2 at 4.5 V via synergistic modification of Mg2+ ion doping and Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 coating doi:10.1007/s42114-025-01373-3
papergraph.abstract: Increasing the operating voltage can significantly increase the energy density of LiCoO2, but it is accompanied by severe structural damage and interface degradation. Especially at a high voltage above 4.5 V, LiCoO2 faces severe challenges, such as irreversible phase transition, lattice oxygen loss, and electrode–electrolyte interface evolution. Herein, a high-voltage LiCoO2 cathode material with bulk doping and surface coating synergistic modification was designed and prepared. The doped Mg2+ ions entered the lithium layers as pillars, stabilizing the layered framework. Meanwhile, Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 was uniformly coated on the surface of LiCoO2, which is beneficial for suppressing Co3+/2+ ion leaching and interface side reactions during cycling. Furthermore, Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 is a fast ion conductor, which facilitates the rapid transport of lithium ions on the surface of the cathode material. Owing to the synergistic effect of Mg2+ ion doping and Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 coating, the orbitals of Co 3d and O 2p in LiCoO2 crystal are changed, and the irreversible phase transition and oxygen loss are inhibited. The modified material exhibited an excellent electrochemical performance. A discharge capacity of 175.5 mAh·g−1 at 10 C rate, as well as a capacity retention of 90.14% after 200 cycles at 1 C rate, was achieved in the modified material. This work supplies a new method for developing high-voltage oxide cathodes through bulk and interfacial modification.

keynotes.title: Pushing Lithium Cobalt Oxides to 4.7 V by Lattice‐Matched Interfacial Engineering

keynote.title: Pushing Lithium Cobalt Oxides to 4.7 V by Lattice‐Matched Interfacial Engineering
keynote.core_problem: Severe structural and interfacial degradation of LiCoO2 above 4.55 V, limiting practical energy density of lithium-ion batteries.
contributions: Rational design of a lattice-matched LiCoPO4 coating for high-voltage LiCoO2
Demonstration of epitaxial, strongly bonded, and fully covering LiCoPO4 coating via in situ chemical reaction
Suppression of oxygen release and irreversible phase transition via P–O tetrahedron configuration at interface
Achievement of stable cycling at 4.6 V (87% capacity retention after 300 cycles) and operation up to 4.7 V/30 °C or 4.6 V/55 °C
methods: In situ chemical reaction to derive LiCoPO4 coating on LiCoO2
Lattice-matched epitaxial growth of LiCoPO4 on LiCoO2 crystallites
Electrochemical testing of LiCoPO4-LiCoO2ǁLi cells under high-voltage and elevated-temperature conditions
experiments_or_evidence: 87% capacity retention after 300 cycles at 4.6 V vs Li/Li+
Stable operation at 4.6 V and 55 °C
Stable operation at 4.7 V and 30 °C

gap_signals.text: LiCoO2 suffers from severe structural and interfacial degradation at >4.55 V.

important_claims.claim: LiCoPO4 coating grows epitaxially on LiCoO2 with strong bonding and complete coverage.
important_claims.evidence: Derived by in situ chemical reaction; ensures stable cathode–electrolyte interface with fewer side reactions and alleviated intergranular cracking and phase collapse.

important_claims.claim: P–O tetrahedron configuration at interface decreases surface oxygen activity of LiCoO2.
important_claims.evidence: Effectively suppresses oxygen release and irreversible phase transition.

important_claims.claim: LiCoPO4-LiCoO2ǁLi cells achieve excellent high-voltage stability.
important_claims.evidence: 87% capacity retention after 300 cycles at 4.6 V; stable operation at 4.6 V/55 °C or 4.7 V/30 °C.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable lattice-matching interfacial engineering strategy applicable to other high-voltage layered oxide cathodes beyond LiCoO2.

keynotes.title: Hierarchical Garnet‐Type Interfacial Engineering Enables Reversible Oxygen Redox in Li‐Rich Layered Cathodes

keynote.title: Hierarchical Garnet‐Type Interfacial Engineering Enables Reversible Oxygen Redox in Li‐Rich Layered Cathodes
keynote.core_problem: Irreversible oxygen release and interfacial degradation in Li-rich layered oxides (LROs), causing voltage decay, capacity fading, and gas evolution.
contributions: In situ construction of a garnet-type Li5La3Nb2O12 (LLNO) fast-ion conductor coating on LRO particles
Multifunctional interfacial engineering unifying coating, Nb5+ doping, and surface reconstruction
Formation of a hierarchical architecture comprising LLNO coating, spinel transition layer, and gradient oxygen-vacancy distribution
Stabilization of reversible oxygen-anion redox via Nb5+–O bonding and oxygen vacancies
methods: Sol–gel process using C4H4NNbO9·nH2O as Nb precursor
In situ pyrolysis with NH3 acting as mild reducing agent
Surface reconstruction inducing near-surface Nb5+ doping and spinel layer formation
Gradient oxygen-vacancy distribution engineering
experiments_or_evidence: 281.7 mAh g−1 discharge capacity at 0.1 C
97.6% capacity retention after 200 cycles at 1 C
Performance comparison against pristine LRO counterpart showing superiority

gap_signals.text: Li-rich layered oxides suffer from irreversible oxygen release and interfacial degradation.
Prior strategies lack integration of coating, doping, and surface reconstruction into a single hierarchical interfacial design.

important_claims.claim: The hierarchical architecture enables reversible oxygen-anion redox.
important_claims.evidence: Attributed to Nb5+–O bonding and oxygen vacancies stabilizing oxygen redox.

important_claims.claim: The optimized cathode outperforms the pristine counterpart.
important_claims.evidence: 281.7 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C and 97.6% capacity retention after 200 cycles at 1 C.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable multifunctional interfacial-engineering strategy for stabilizing anion redox in other oxygen-redox-active cathodes.

keynotes.title: Surface Reconstruction Enables High‐Voltage, Long‐Life All‐Solid‐State Batteries

keynote.title: Surface Reconstruction Enables High‐Voltage, Long‐Life All‐Solid‐State Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Voltage limitation (≤4.3 V) in halide-based all-solid-state batteries causes structural degradation of layered oxide cathodes (e.g., LiCoO2) and cathode electrolyte interface (CEI) deterioration, restricting energy density and cycle life.
contributions: Atomic-scale surface reconstruction of LiCoO2 into a perovskite-phase nanolayer via limited Li+/La3+ ion exchange
Stabilization of the cathode electrolyte interface (CEI) and suppression of lattice oxygen release
Enabling stable cycling at high cut-off voltage (4.6 V) with long cycle life (>1000 cycles) and high areal capacity (5 mAh cm−2)
methods: Limited Li+/La3+ ion exchange to reconstruct LiCoO2 surface into perovskite-phase nanolayer
Interface engineering to suppress lattice oxygen release and stabilize CEI
experiments_or_evidence: High specific capacity of 208 mAh g−1 at 4.6 V
Initial coulombic efficiency of 97.4% at 4.6 V
Long-term cycling stability: >1000 cycles
94% capacity retention after 200 cycles at 5 mAh cm−2 areal loading

gap_signals.text: Elevated cut-off voltages cause structural degradation of layered oxides and CEI deterioration — implying lack of robust interfacial stabilization strategies.
Current voltage limitations (≤4.3 V) restrict advancement of energy density in halide-based ASSBs.

important_claims.claim: Engineered perovskite-phase nanolayer suppresses lattice oxygen release and stabilizes the CEI.
important_claims.evidence: Prevents both bulk-phase collapse and decomposition of Li3InCl6 electrolyte; enables stable cycling at 4.6 V and >1000 cycles.

important_claims.claim: Surface reconstruction enables high-voltage operation (4.6 V) with high capacity and long cycle life.
important_claims.evidence: 208 mAh g−1 specific capacity, 97.4% initial coulombic efficiency, 94% retention after 200 cycles at 5 mAh cm−2.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable atomic-scale interface engineering strategy for stabilizing layered oxide cathodes in high-voltage halide-based ASSBs; methodology (ion-exchange-driven surface phase transformation) may extend to other oxide cathodes or anion-stable electrolytes.

keynotes.title: Subcritical Hydrothermal Synthesis in a Water-Alcohol Co-solvent of Size-Controlled High-Entropy Spinel Oxide Nanoparticles as Oxygen Evolution Electrocatalysts

keynote.title: Subcritical Hydrothermal Synthesis in a Water-Alcohol Co-solvent of Size-Controlled High-Entropy Spinel Oxide Nanoparticles as Oxygen Evolution Electrocatalysts
keynote.core_problem: Low-temperature synthesis of size-controlled, single-phase, crystalline high-entropy oxide (HEO) nanoparticles is challenging due to mismatched hydrolysis behaviors of multiple metal species, hindering simultaneous nucleation and crystallization.
contributions: An alcohol-assisted subcritical hydrothermal strategy for synthesizing size-controlled HEO nanoparticles (Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Zn) in spinel structure
Demonstration that water–alcohol co-solvent composition tunes particle size (17.2 nm → 10.0 nm) without altering bulk metal composition
Evidence that smaller HEO nanoparticles exhibit enhanced alkaline oxygen evolution reaction (OER) activity via increased accessible active sites
methods: Subcritical hydrothermal synthesis using water–alcohol co-solvent
Systematic tuning of water–alcohol ratio to control nucleation and growth
Structural characterization (implied: XRD, TEM/HRTEM, EDS or EELS for phase purity, crystallinity, elemental distribution, local coordination)
experiments_or_evidence: Particle size reduction from 17.2 nm to 10.0 nm with increasing alcohol fraction
Retention of single-phase spinel structure across sizes (confirmed by structural characterization)
Homogeneous elemental distribution and similar local coordination environments
Enhanced OER activity for smaller nanoparticles
Nearly unchanged Tafel slopes, indicating mechanism preservation
assumptions: Size-dependent OER enhancement arises primarily from increased electrochemically active surface area, not intrinsic activity change
Lowered dielectric constant of water–alcohol co-solvent drives rapid nucleation and suppresses coarsening

gap_signals.text: No simple solution-based route previously existed for nanoscale control of crystalline multicomponent oxide electrocatalysts.

Low-temperature synthesis of size-controlled, single-phase, and crystalline HEO nanoparticles remains challenging because multicomponent oxide formation requires simultaneous nucleation and crystallization of multiple metal species with different hydrolysis behaviors.

important_claims.claim: Smaller HEO nanoparticles exhibit enhanced alkaline OER activity.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental observation of improved activity correlated with size reduction; nearly unchanged Tafel slopes support increased active site density rather than mechanistic change.

important_claims.claim: Size reduction is attributed to lowered dielectric constant of water–alcohol co-solvent, promoting rapid nucleation and suppressing coarsening.
important_claims.evidence: Particle size decreased systematically with co-solvent composition; structural integrity and compositional fidelity were maintained, supporting solvent-property-driven kinetic control.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable, low-temperature, solution-based synthetic lever (co-solvent dielectric tuning) for size control of multicomponent oxide nanomaterials—potentially extensible to other HEOs or complex oxides requiring kinetic stabilization of metastable nanoscale phases.

keynotes.title: Subcritical Hydrothermal Synthesis in a Water-Alcohol Co-solvent of Size-Controlled High-Entropy Spinel Oxide Nanoparticles as Oxygen Evolution Electrocatalysts

keynote.title: Subcritical Hydrothermal Synthesis in a Water-Alcohol Co-solvent of Size-Controlled High-Entropy Spinel Oxide Nanoparticles as Oxygen Evolution Electrocatalysts
keynote.core_problem: Difficulty in synthesizing size-controlled, single-phase high-entropy oxide (HEO) nanoparticles—particularly spinel-based HEO NPs—with tunable crystallite size while preserving phase purity and composition.
contributions: A methanol–water subcritical hydrothermal synthesis strategy enabling precise control of spinel HEO NP size (7.5–13.3 nm) without compositional deviation.
Demonstration that reduced particle size increases specific surface area and significantly enhances OER activity.
Mechanistic insight linking solvent dielectric constant reduction to burst nucleation and suppressed Ostwald ripening.
methods: Subcritical hydrothermal synthesis using water–methanol co-solvent.
Systematic variation of methanol content to tune crystallite size.
XRD for phase identification and crystallite size estimation (Scherrer equation).
TEM/HRTEM for morphology and particle size distribution.
XPS and EDX for elemental composition and oxidation state analysis.
Electrochemical OER evaluation (LSV, Tafel, EIS, stability testing) in alkaline medium.
experiments_or_evidence: Crystallite size tuned from 13.3 nm to 7.5 nm via methanol content; confirmed by XRD and TEM.
Single-phase spinel structure maintained across all sizes (XRD pattern matching, no impurity peaks).
Uniform elemental distribution confirmed by EDX mapping.
OER overpotential decreased and Tafel slope improved with smaller particle size.
Increased electrochemical surface area (ECSA) correlated with reduced particle size.
assumptions: Particle size reduction directly increases active site density via increased specific surface area.
Suppressed Ostwald ripening under low-dielectric co-solvent conditions is sufficient to kinetically trap small nuclei.
Spinel phase stability and cation distribution are preserved across the synthesized size range.
limitations: Limited to first-row transition metals (Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu); generalizability to heavier or redox-sensitive elements untested.
No long-term operational stability data beyond short-term chronoamperometry.
Scalability and reproducibility across reactor batches not reported.
No in situ/operando characterization to confirm active phase or surface reconstruction during OER.

gap_signals.text: Limited to first-row transition metals (Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu); generalizability to heavier or redox-sensitive elements untested.
No in situ/operando characterization to confirm active phase or surface reconstruction during OER.
No long-term operational stability data beyond short-term chronoamperometry.
Scalability and reproducibility across reactor batches not reported.
Few reports link systematic HEO NP size tuning to quantitative OER metrics (overpotential, turnover frequency).
Role of solvent dielectric constant in nucleation kinetics of multi-cation oxide systems remains underexplored.
Prior HEO syntheses lack precise size control without phase segregation or compositional drift.

datasets_or_materials: Synthesized spinel HEO NPs: MnFeCoNiCuO₄ (nominal stoichiometry).
Reference catalysts: RuO₂, IrO₂, and commercial Pt/C (for benchmarking).

important_claims.claim: Reducing solvent dielectric constant via methanol addition promotes burst nucleation and suppresses Ostwald ripening.
important_claims.evidence: Systematic crystallite size reduction (13.3 → 7.5 nm) with increasing methanol content, coupled with uniform particle size distributions in TEM and absence of larger secondary particles.

important_claims.claim: Smaller HEO NPs exhibit enhanced OER activity due to increased specific surface area.
important_claims.evidence: Monotonic improvement in overpotential and Tafel slope correlating with decreasing size; ECSA increase confirmed via double-layer capacitance measurements.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a transferable, non-toxic, scalable hydrothermal protocol for size-resolved HEO nanomaterial synthesis—enabling structure–activity studies across entropy-stabilized oxides and facilitating catalyst optimization beyond OER (e.g., ORR, CO2RR).

keynotes.title: Onset potential for electrolyte oxidation and Ni-rich cathode degradation in lithium-ion batteries

keynote.title: Onset potential for electrolyte oxidation and Ni-rich cathode degradation in lithium-ion batteries
keynote.core_problem: Poor cycling performance of Ni-rich cathodes (e.g., NMC811) at high voltages (>4.4 V vs. Li/Li+) due to synergistic degradation involving lattice oxygen release and ethylene carbonate (EC)-mediated electrolyte oxidation.
contributions: Identifies 4.4 V vs. Li/Li+ as the onset potential for lattice oxygen release in NMC811, triggering accelerated degradation.
Demonstrates that EC-containing electrolytes exacerbate oxygen loss, electrolyte oxidation, and cathode surface degradation above this onset potential.
Shows contrast with NMC111 — which does not release oxygen up to 4.6 V — where electrolyte composition (EC vs. EC-free) does not significantly affect degradation extent.
Highlights fundamental incompatibility between conventional EC-based electrolytes and oxygen-releasing cathodes (including Li-/Mn-rich and disordered rocksalt cathodes).
methods: Electrochemical cycling of NMC811 and NMC111 cathodes in EC-containing vs. EC-free electrolytes.
Monitoring degradation above 4.4 V vs. Li/Li+ to correlate with lattice oxygen release onset.
Comparative analysis of electrolyte oxidation and cathode surface degradation across voltage windows and electrolyte formulations.
experiments_or_evidence: Increased electrolyte oxidation/breakdown and cathode surface degradation observed concurrently above 4.4 V in EC-containing electrolytes for NMC811.
No significant difference in degradation extent between EC-containing and EC-free electrolytes for NMC111 up to 4.6 V.
Correlation between oxygen loss onset (at 4.4 V) and accelerated degradation in NMC811 with EC-containing electrolytes.
assumptions: Lattice oxygen release is the primary trigger for accelerated interfacial degradation in Ni-rich cathodes above 4.4 V.
EC’s reactivity toward reactive oxygen species or oxidized cathode surfaces drives additional parasitic reactions.
NMC111 serves as a suitable control due to its structural stability and absence of oxygen release up to 4.6 V.

gap_signals.text: Motivates further work on wider classes of electrolytes and additives.
Focuses only on EC-containing vs. EC-free comparisons; no exploration of specific alternative solvents or additive mechanisms.

important_claims.claim: The onset potential for lattice oxygen release in NMC811 is 4.4 V vs. Li/Li+.
important_claims.evidence: Oxygen loss is enhanced by EC-containing electrolytes compared to EC-free, and correlates with increased electrolyte oxidation and cathode surface degradation above 4.4 V.

important_claims.claim: EC-containing electrolytes are incompatible with oxygen-releasing cathodes.
important_claims.evidence: Degradation accelerates above 4.4 V specifically in EC-containing electrolytes for NMC811, whereas NMC111 — which does not release oxygen up to 4.6 V — shows similar degradation regardless of EC presence.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides voltage- and chemistry-specific failure thresholds (4.4 V onset) and a controlled comparative framework (NMC811 vs. NMC111, EC vs. EC-free) for evaluating electrolyte-cathode compatibility; useful for designing stable high-voltage electrolytes and screening next-generation cathodes.

methods: Machine-learning-potential-assisted molecular dynamics simulations
Enhanced sampling techniques

important_claims.claim: Formation of two adjacent Co vacancies (Co vacancy cluster) is necessary for oxygen dimer generation.

important_claims.claim: Ti doping significantly increases the kinetic barrier of Co ion migration and thus suppresses Co vacancy cluster formation.
important_claims.evidence: Simulation results showing altered migration energetics under Ti doping.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a simulation-ready mechanistic framework for probing vacancy-mediated oxygen evolution in layered oxide cathodes; methodology transferable to other TM oxides (e.g., NMC, LMO) and dopant screening.

important_claims.claim: Ti doping effectively suppresses Co vacancy cluster formation.
important_claims.evidence: Reduced frequency/likelihood of adjacent Co vacancy formation observed in Ti-doped simulations.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a validated simulation framework (ML-PMD + enhanced sampling) and mechanistic insight (vacancy-cluster–dimer coupling) applicable to stability analysis of other layered oxide cathodes (e.g., NMC, NCA); identifies Co vacancy clustering as a quantifiable proxy for oxygen instability.

keynotes.title: Monitoring electrode/electrolyte interfaces of Li-ion batteries under working conditions: A surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopic study on LiCoO2 composite cathodes

keynote.title: Monitoring electrode/electrolyte interfaces of Li-ion batteries under working conditions: A surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopic study on LiCoO2 composite cathodes
keynote.core_problem: Electrode degradation and stability issues limit the application of lithium-ion batteries.
contributions: Demonstration of surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) using Au@SiO2 nanoparticles to monitor electrode/electrolyte interfaces in operando.
Observation of Li2CO3 (at 1090 cm⁻¹) as an intermediate SEI component on LiCoO2 composite cathodes during electrochemical cycling.
Establishment of a spectroscopic method linking electrochemical performance with structural/interface changes in working batteries.
methods: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) with Au@SiO2 nanoparticles (Au core, thin SiO2 shell).
In situ or operando Raman monitoring during electrochemical cycling of LiCoO2 composite cathodes.
experiments_or_evidence: Detection of Li2CO3 Raman signal at 1090 cm⁻¹ during cycling.
Correlation of Li2CO3 formation/decomposition dynamics with electrochemical cycling behavior.

gap_signals.text: Need for spectroscopic techniques capable of monitoring electrode/electrolyte interfaces under working conditions.
Electrode degradation and stability issues still limit Li-ion battery application.

important_claims.claim: Li2CO3 forms as an intermediate SEI component on LiCoO2 composite cathodes during electrochemical cycling.
important_claims.evidence: Surface-enhanced Raman signal of Li2CO3 observed at 1090 cm⁻¹ during cycling.

important_claims.claim: Raman spectroscopy—enhanced via Au@SiO2—can monitor electrode/electrolyte interfaces under working conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Successful detection of dynamic Li2CO3 formation/decomposition correlated with cycling.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a validated SERS platform (Au@SiO2) and protocol for operando interfacial monitoring of cathode SEI chemistry; enables future studies of SEI evolution across cathode chemistries or electrolytes.

keynotes.title: Ultra-high areal capacity Li electrodeposition at metal-solid electrolyte interfaces under minimal stack pressures enabled by interfacial Na-K liquids

keynote.title: Ultra-high areal capacity Li electrodeposition at metal-solid electrolyte interfaces under minimal stack pressures enabled by interfacial Na-K liquids
keynote.core_problem: Impedance growth at the Li metal–solid electrolyte interface due to void formation during cycling at practical current densities (>0.5 mA cm⁻²) and areal capacities (>1.5 mAh cm⁻²), limiting solid-state Li metal battery performance.
contributions: Demonstration of reversible Li stripping/plating up to 30 mAh cm⁻² (≈150 μm Li) at >0.5 mA cm⁻² and <75 kPa stack pressure
Introduction of a Na-K liquid interfacial film to enable stable Li metal–LLZTO interface
Causal evidence that Na-K liquid at the Li stripping interface—not just plating—is essential for high areal capacity operation
methods: Interfacial engineering using Na-K eutectic liquid (liquid at room temperature) as a wetting layer between Li metal and LLZTO solid electrolyte
Electrochemical cycling (stripping/plating) under controlled stack pressure (<75 kPa)
Impedance monitoring during cycling to assess interfacial stability
experiments_or_evidence: Achievement of 30 mAh cm⁻² areal capacity with minimal impedance change over cycling
Comparison showing performance collapse in absence of Na-K liquid under identical conditions
Demonstration of reversibility at current densities >0.5 mA cm⁻² and low stack pressure
assumptions: Na-K liquid remains chemically and electrochemically stable at the Li/LLZTO interface during operation
Li metal maintains intimate contact with LLZTO via Na-K wetting without side reactions dominating
Void formation is primarily mitigated by interfacial fluidity and improved Li⁺ transport kinetics, not bulk mechanical compliance alone
limitations: Long-term cycling stability (e.g., >100 cycles) not reported
Na-K reactivity with ambient atmosphere or trace moisture not addressed
Scalability of Na-K interfacial integration into full cells not demonstrated
Temperature dependence of Na-K liquid stability and performance not characterized

gap_signals.text: Long-term cycling stability (e.g., >100 cycles) not reported
Na-K reactivity with ambient atmosphere or trace moisture not addressed
Scalability of Na-K interfacial integration into full cells not demonstrated
Temperature dependence of Na-K liquid stability and performance not characterized
Prior approaches rely on high stack pressure or compliant interlayers; this work achieves high areal capacity under minimal pressure via liquid interfacial mediation.
Void formation at Li–solid electrolyte interface under practical current densities and areal capacities remains unresolved.

important_claims.claim: Na-K liquid at the Li stripping interface enables high areal capacity operation (>30 mAh cm⁻²) at >0.5 mA cm⁻² and <75 kPa.
important_claims.evidence: Performance collapses in its absence under identical conditions; impedance remains stable only when Na-K is present.

important_claims.claim: The approach permits ≈10× higher areal capacity than today’s lithium-ion batteries.
important_claims.evidence: Reported 30 mAh cm⁻² vs. typical commercial Li-ion areal capacities of ~3–4 mAh cm⁻².

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable interfacial design principle—using low-melting-point, ion-conducting liquid metals—to decouple interfacial contact maintenance from mechanical stack pressure, applicable to other alkali-metal/solid-electrolyte systems (e.g., Na, K).

keynotes.title: Dynamic surface reconstruction of perovskite oxides in oxygen evolution reaction and its impacts on catalysis: a critical review

keynote.title: Dynamic surface reconstruction of perovskite oxides in oxygen evolution reaction and its impacts on catalysis: a critical review
keynote.core_problem: The active structure and corresponding catalytic mechanism of perovskite oxides in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) remain elusive, due to dynamic surface reconstruction under operational conditions and mechanistic complexity.
contributions: A critical review synthesizing in-situ and operando characterization studies (last decade) on surface dynamic structural evolution of perovskite oxides during OER.
Overview of reported active structures for perovskite oxides.
Guiding principles for designing highly active and stable OER catalysts based on surface reconstruction insights.

gap_signals.text: Intricate surface reconstruction under working conditions complicates mechanistic understanding.
Identification of the active structure and corresponding catalytic mechanism for perovskite oxides in OER remains elusive.

important_claims.claim: Perovskite oxides exhibit dynamic surface reconstruction during OER that critically impacts catalytic activity.
important_claims.evidence: Synthesis of in-situ and operando characterization studies over the last decade focusing on surface evolution.

important_claims.claim: Active structures on perovskite oxides are not static and differ from bulk or as-synthesized phases.
important_claims.evidence: Review concludes with an overview of reported active structures, implying deviation from pristine surfaces.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a curated foundation for researchers to interpret operando data, select appropriate characterization methods, and rationally design reconstruction-resilient or reconstruction-leveraging perovskite OER catalysts.

keynotes.title: Optimizing the electrochemical performance of LiCoO2 at 4.5 V via synergistic modification of Mg2+ ion doping and Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 coating

keynote.title: Optimizing the electrochemical performance of LiCoO2 at 4.5 V via synergistic modification of Mg2+ ion doping and Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 coating
keynote.core_problem: Structural damage and interface degradation in LiCoO2 cathodes operating above 4.5 V, including irreversible phase transition, lattice oxygen loss, and electrode–electrolyte interface evolution.
contributions: Design and synthesis of a synergistically modified LiCoO2 cathode with Mg2+ bulk doping and Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 surface coating
Demonstration that Mg2+ doping stabilizes the layered framework by acting as pillars in lithium layers
Demonstration that Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 coating suppresses Co3+/2+ leaching and interfacial side reactions while enhancing Li+ transport
Evidence that orbital modulation (Co 3d and O 2p) underlies suppression of irreversible phase transition and oxygen loss
methods: Mg2+ ion doping into LiCoO2 bulk structure
Uniform surface coating with Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 (LLTP)
Electrochemical testing: galvanostatic charge/discharge at 10 C and 1 C rates
Structural and interfacial characterization (implied but not specified: e.g., XRD, XPS, TEM — not named in text)
experiments_or_evidence: Discharge capacity of 175.5 mAh·g−1 at 10 C rate
Capacity retention of 90.14% after 200 cycles at 1 C rate
Inhibition of irreversible phase transition and oxygen loss (attributed to orbital changes in Co 3d and O 2p)
assumptions: Mg2+ ions occupy lithium-layer sites and act as structural pillars
Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 coating is ionically conductive and chemically stable at high voltage
Orbital modulation (Co 3d and O 2p) induced by dual modification is causally linked to suppressed degradation

gap_signals.text: Prior work lacks synergistic bulk doping + fast-ion-conducting surface coating for simultaneous structural and interfacial stabilization
High-voltage LiCoO2 (>4.5 V) suffers from irreversible phase transition, lattice oxygen loss, and interface degradation — unresolved by prior single-modification strategies

important_claims.claim: Mg2+ doping stabilizes the layered framework by entering lithium layers as pillars
important_claims.evidence: Described as mechanism enabling structural stability; no direct experimental evidence (e.g., site occupancy quantification) provided in excerpt

important_claims.claim: Li1.3La0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 coating suppresses Co3+/2+ leaching and interface side reactions
important_claims.evidence: Stated as functional benefit; no spectroscopic or dissolution data cited in excerpt

important_claims.claim: Dual modification changes Co 3d and O 2p orbitals, inhibiting irreversible phase transition and oxygen loss
important_claims.evidence: Attributed to synergistic effect; no orbital-resolved spectroscopy (e.g., XAS, EELS) or DFT evidence described in excerpt

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a reusable dual-modification design principle (bulk pillar dopant + fast-ion-conducting coating) for high-voltage layered oxide cathodes; LLTP coating and Mg2+ doping are transferable to other systems like NMC or Li-rich oxides.
